metadata.split_role: Expanded porcini, Tylopilus, dark-pored, and brown bolete records.
metadata.notes: Physical data split only. Species record bodies were preserved; each canonical slug appears in exactly one base mushroom file. v4.2.81 added species-specific bolete notes/source links and simplified review reasons for selected records. Updated in v4.2.82 with 25-record mushroom credibility pass including Black Velvet Bolete and Blue-staining Slippery Jack. v4.2.83: mushroom bolete photo/credibility batch; Commons images added for Neoporphyrellus alboater and Suillus tomentosus; 25-record Suillus/Leccinum/Neoporphyrellus pass. v4.2.85 refreshed Black Velvet Bolete image URLs to direct upload.wikimedia.org files and carried forward v4.2.83 image coverage. v4.2.86: optional photo patches are treated as fill-only for existing base-record image fields; direct image fallback hardened for Black Velvet Bolete and Suillus tomentosus where applicable. v4.2.87-r2026-04-30-mushroom-suillus-leccinum-credibility4: carried forward with patch-precedence-safe image handling. v4.2.88: 25-record mushroom credibility/review pass; porcini/Tylopilus/Xerocomus records rechecked and remaining red/caution records improved while preserving true image/local-taxonomy review flags. v4.2.99-r2026-05-11-fold-mushroom-photo-patches1: folded mushroom photo patch records directly into canonical source records and retired runtime PHOTO_PATCH_PATHS. v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split active data sources by size; record bodies and source order preserved.

review_reason_audit.rule: Normalize explicit review signals into review_reasons/review_note and prefix notes for Needs Review list visibility.

metadata.split_note: v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split this active data source by file size to keep working chunks under roughly 75–100 KB. Record bodies and source order were preserved.

records.culinary_uses: Some Gyroporus chestnut boletes are edible, but this guide treats the borealis/smithii record conservatively until the species split is better confirmed regionally.
records.notes: Chestnut-type Gyroporus: brittle hollow stem, chestnut-brown cap, and yellowish spore-print group; treat species boundary as conservative.

mushroom_profile.summary: Chestnut-type Gyroporus: brittle hollow stem, chestnut-brown cap, and yellowish spore-print group; treat species boundary as conservative.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.
Needs image coverage.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Gyroporus records should be checked for brittle/hollow stem tissue, dry cap, lack of strong reticulation, and association with hardwoods or mixed woods.
Species-specific notes and source links updated in v4.2.82 mushroom batch.
v4.2.88: review reasons rechecked; remaining blocker kept as image/local-taxonomy issue rather than source/species-note cleanup.

records.edibility_detail: Potentially edible group, but keep as cautious because the app record represents a borealis/smithii concept rather than a crisp single field-confirmed species.

records.field_identification: Gyroporus records should be checked for brittle/hollow stem tissue, dry cap, lack of strong reticulation, and association with hardwoods or mixed woods.

records.culinary_uses: Not a prime food mushroom in this guide; keep as a cautious/ID record unless a local expert confirms the collection.
records.notes: Dusky Bolete: dark brown to gray-brown bolete with brownish pore surface and darkening/staining tendencies; more ID interest than table prize.

mushroom_profile.summary: Dusky Bolete: dark brown to gray-brown bolete with brownish pore surface and darkening/staining tendencies; more ID interest than table prize.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.
Needs image coverage.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Check for a dusky cap, brownish pore surface, dark spore-print group, and flesh/stem staining tendencies. It is easy to confuse with other dark Tylopilus/Porphyrellus-like boletes.
Species-specific notes and source links updated in v4.2.82 mushroom batch.
v4.2.88: review reasons rechecked; remaining blocker kept as image/local-taxonomy issue rather than source/species-note cleanup.

records.edibility_detail: Treat conservatively. Dark boletes in this group need careful separation before any food use.

records.mushroom_card_note: Dusky Bolete: dark brown to gray-brown bolete with brownish pore surface and darkening/staining tendencies; more ID interest than table prize.
records.field_identification: Check for a dusky cap, brownish pore surface, dark spore-print group, and flesh/stem staining tendencies. It is easy to confuse with other dark Tylopilus/Porphyrellus-like boletes.

records.culinary_uses: Do not collect as food. Its importance here is as a caution/look-alike for king boletes.
records.notes: False King Bolete: a dangerous king-bolete look-alike concern in the Great Lakes region; do not treat as porcini.

mushroom_profile.summary: False King Bolete: a dangerous king-bolete look-alike concern in the Great Lakes region; do not treat as porcini.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall, Great Lakes/eastern North America.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan / Upper Midwest bolete pass.
Needs image coverage.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Compare any “king bolete” carefully against Boletus huronensis: duller cap, yellowish pores at maturity, blue staining in some tissues, and Great Lakes reports matter.

records.culinary_uses: Not a prime edible in this guide; mostly a recognizable hardwood bolete for comparison.
records.notes: Gray Bolete: drab gray oak/hardwood bolete with a coarsely reticulate stem that yellows from the base upward.

mushroom_profile.summary: Gray Bolete: drab gray oak/hardwood bolete with a coarsely reticulate stem that yellows from the base upward.

Needs source links and fuller field notes.

records.culinary_uses: Mild Tylopilus species are sometimes eaten, but this one is not a beginner target and must be separated from bitter Tylopilus species.
records.notes: Indecisus Bolete: mild Tylopilus with pinkish mature pores that bruise brown and a mostly whitish, non-olive stem.

mushroom_profile.summary: Indecisus Bolete: mild Tylopilus with pinkish mature pores that bruise brown and a mostly whitish, non-olive stem.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall, eastern North America.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan / Upper Midwest bolete pass.
Needs image coverage.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Key checks: pinkish mature pore surface bruising brown, mild taste, soft flesh, fairly equal whitish stem, and hardwood/oak association.

records.edibility_detail: Edible reports exist for mild Tylopilus, but treat this as a cautious/advanced ID mushroom.

records.culinary_uses: Not a prime food target in this guide; included for completeness and comparison.
records.notes: Leather-colored Bolete: brown/yellow-pored hardwood bolete; keep as a regional comparison rather than a prime edible.

mushroom_profile.summary: Leather-colored Bolete: brown/yellow-pored hardwood bolete; keep as a regional comparison rather than a prime edible.

Use brown leather-colored cap, yellowish pore surface, hardwood context, and lack of strong dangerous-color signals to compare against porcini-like boletes.

records.edibility_detail: Treat as occasional/niche until local ID confidence improves.

records.culinary_uses: Porcini-group edible when confidently identified and in good condition; keep separate from bitter Tylopilus and other brown boletes.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Lilac Bolete. Added in the Michigan audit pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
staining: Flesh not staining; pores may bruise deeper yellow or brownish
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall under oaks and other hardwoods.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan bolete audit.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.
pore_color: White young; yellow to brownish yellow mature

records.edibility_detail: Confirm yellow mature pores without olive shades, liver-red/lilac-brown tones, oak/hardwood setting, and non-staining white flesh.
records.field_identification: Oak/hardwood bolete with young lilac-brown to liver-red tones, yellow mature pore surface lacking olive shades, stem often flushed with liver color, reticulation possible, and white flesh that does not stain.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus separans
use_links.notes: Oak/hardwood association, yellow pores, non-staining flesh, and liver-red stem flush.

records.edibility_notes: Porcini-group edible when confidently identified and in good condition; keep separate from bitter Tylopilus and other brown boletes.

records.notes: Ornate Bolete: yellow to brown cap and bright yellow, strongly netted stem; pretty, distinctive, and sometimes bitter.
